1:
The witnesses take this opening verse — Jesus keeping to Galilee “because the Jews sought to kill him” — as a study in the union of Christ’s divine power and his real humanity. The Latin fathers press the point most fully. Augustine says that here especially “our Lord Jesus Christ has most especially commended Himself to our faith in respect of His humanity”: he who “while remaining God… took manhood” now conceals himself, yet “must not be thought to have lost His power, but only to have offered an example to our infirmity” — “the Head began with doing Himself, what He sanctioned in the member,” that his faithful ones might lawfully hide from persecution “that no guilt might attach to such concealment.” Bede adds the geography, that Judea and Galilee are divisions of Palestine, the former named from Judah the royal tribe.

The Eastern Chrysostom wrestles with the paradox of the older reading, “he had not power to walk in Jewry.” “What sayest thou, O blessed John? Had not He ‘power,’ who was able to do all that He would?” — he who cast the soldiers backward (John 18:6) and would soon teach openly in the temple in the midst of the feast? His answer is that the words bespeak not weakness but Christ’s display of “the attribute both of deity and of manhood,” accommodating himself to our condition. He names the root of the whole hostility: “nothing is worse than envy and malice; through these death entered into the world” (Wisdom 2:24) — the same root that slew Abel and hunted David, and now makes the Jews “Christ-slayers.”

The Reformed and Puritan voices make the same resolution practically. Calvin says Christ “determined to confine himself within the limits which belonged to him as man,” having “emptied himself” (Philippians 2:6–8), and “conducted himself as a man who was liable to dangers.” Henry insists the text says “not… He durst not, but, He would not walk in Jewry” — not cowardice but prudence, “because his hour was not yet come” — and draws the double lesson that “Gospel light is justly taken away from those that endeavour to extinguish it,” while “in times of imminent peril it is… advisable to withdraw and abscond for our own safety.”

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the frame. The phrase “after these things” is John’s indefinite note of order (Robertson), here skipping “the period of withdrawal from Galilee of about six months, from passover to tabernacles”; the imperfect “walked” (periepatei) is “a literal picture of the itinerant ministry of Jesus,” and the imperfect “sought to kill” marks a settled, progressive hostility traceable to John 5:18. Barnes notes it was “the rulers of the Jews,” not the common people, who sought his life.
2:
The witnesses pause to describe the feast that frames the chapter, and the grammatical-historical commentators do it thoroughly. The feast of tabernacles (Robertson notes the technical name skēnopēgia, found only here in the New Testament) began on the fifteenth of Tisri, answering to our September — about five months after the Passover of John 6:4 — and lasted seven days, with an eighth “great day of the feast” (John 7:37) added in later times. During it all Israel dwelt in booths “in remembrance of their dwelling in tabernacles when they came out of the land of Egypt” (Ellicott, citing Leviticus 23:34, 23:42–43; Nehemiah 8:16–18). It was one of the three annual feasts at which every male was required to appear before the Lord (Exodus 23:14; Deuteronomy 16:13–16), at once a memorial of the wilderness deliverance and a harvest thanksgiving; Josephus, several note, calls it “the holiest and greatest of the feasts.” Clarke and Lightfoot add the later ceremonies — the branches of palm, willow, and myrtle carried and waved, the circuits of the altar — that will give point to Christ’s cry on the last day (v.37).

Gill draws the typology dear to the older expositors: the feast “was typical of Christ’s tabernacling in human nature,” and “an emblem of the saints dwelling in the earthly houses and tabernacles of their bodies, in this their wilderness and pilgrimage state.” Calvin, for the Reformed, keeps to the purpose Moses gives — that “by this annual ceremony the Jews might call to remembrance, that their fathers lived forty years” without houses, “that they might thus celebrate the grace of God displayed in their deliverance” (Leviticus 23:34). He recalls the two reasons Christ usually came to such feasts — that, “being subject to the Law… he wished to omit no part of the observation of it,” and that the great assembly gave “a better opportunity of advancing the Gospel” — and marks how striking it is that “now the Evangelist relates that Christ kept himself in retirement at Galilee, as if he did not intend to come to Jerusalem.”
3:
The brothers’ challenge — “Depart hence, and go into Judea, that thy disciples also may see the works that thou doest” — raises both a question of identity and a reading of motive. On the first, the witnesses divide. The grammatical-historical Robertson takes them as “half-brothers,” hostile to Jesus’ Messianic claims, their advice “clearly ironical” and “impertinence on their part.” Gill insists they are not the apostles James, Joses, Simon, and Jude (who believed and abode to the last) but “some distant relations of Mary or Joseph”; Wesley and Calvin likewise make them cousins, “the sons of his mother’s sister,” since “the Hebrews include all cousins and other relations” under the word “brethren” (compare Genesis 31:32). Clarke leaves it open — they may be “the children of the sisters of his mother,” or, as “some of the ancients” held, Joseph’s children by a former wife — and rebukes those Protestants who, “without any reason,” cling like Papists to a dogma of perpetual virginity.

On the motive all agree the brothers spoke in unbelief and a kind of mockery. Calvin says they “ridicule him, because they do not think that his conduct is rational,” upbraiding him “with folly, because, while he wishes to be something, he wants confidence in himself, and does not venture to appear openly before men.” Ellicott reads the taunt sharply: after the great defection in Galilee (John 6:66) and the quiet months since, the brothers, who “have seen these works and do not believe,” challenge him to “an open demonstration” before the rulers at Jerusalem — “if He is the Messiah, no conspiracy to kill Him can prevail.” Robertson notes they may have heard of the synagogue defection at Capernaum and now press him, with heavy irony, to prove himself where it is most dangerous. The “disciples” they name are those Jesus had made earlier in Judea (John 2:23; John 4:1), now to be judges of his pretensions. Barnes hears in the whole speech the derision Christ bore “from his relatives and friends on account of his pretensions,” a foretaste of what his followers must expect (compare John 15:18–19); and Robertson notes that the brothers, friendly enough at first (John 2:12), have hardened into open hostility to his Messianic claim.
4:
The brothers complete their reasoning — “For there is no man that doeth any thing in secret… If thou do these things, shew thyself to the world” — and the witnesses agree the logic is worldly to its core. The grammatical-historical commentators expose the assumption. Robertson grants it “wise advice in the abstract that a public teacher must allow inspection of his deeds,” but “the motive is evil”; the conditional “if thou do these things” (ei tauta poieis) “assumes the reality of the deeds of Jesus,” yet “the use of the condition at all throws doubt on it.” Lightfoot says they did not believe “because they saw him live so obscure, and did not behave himself with that pomp and outward appearance which they expected in the Messiah,” and urged him to Judea “that, upon the lustre of his miracles, he might shine with greater splendour.” Clarke puts their speech into words: “It is too little to employ thyself… among an ignorant and credulous people… go to Jerusalem, the capital, and among the learned doctors… work thy miracles, and get thyself a name.” Barnes adds that the taunt was “not in sincerity, but in derision,” the common lot of Christians to be mocked “from his relatives and friends.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the moral. The brothers “trampled under foot the manifest proofs of his Divine power, which they ought to have beheld with the greatest admiration”; their counsel reveals “how great is the indolence of men in considering the works of God.” And he notes the painful pattern, true “in daily experience, that the children of God suffer greater annoyance from their near relations than from strangers,” who become “instruments of Satan… tempting sometimes to ambition, and sometimes to avarice.” Ellicott sums the brothers’ creed: the works, if real, “are for the world, and not for the retirement of home” — a verdict that mistakes the very nature of Christ’s mission, which seeks not reputation but the Father’s glory and men’s salvation.

Wesley takes the brothers’ maxim at face value — “no man doth any thing… in secret, but rather desireth to be of public use” — and that, the witnesses agree, is precisely the worldly axiom Christ’s whole ministry overturns: he does his Father’s works not to be seen of men but in obedience to a hidden purpose, and will manifest himself, when his hour comes, on the cross the world counts shame.
5:
John’s flat statement — “For neither did his brethren believe in him” — draws from the witnesses both an historical accounting and a doctrinal lesson. The grammatical-historical commentators take the words in their plain sense: even his brethren “did not at this time believe Him to be the Messiah” (Ellicott). Clarke explains they received him as “an eminent prophet” but reasoned that, were he truly Messiah, “he would wish to manifest himself… to the world.” Robertson hears the imperfect as “a sad picture of the persistent refusal of the brothers,” turned “sarcastic” here, coming after the rejections at Nazareth (Mark 6:1–6; Matthew 13:54–58) and the charge that he was in league with Beelzebub (Mark 3:31–35). Yet Ellicott marks the hopeful sequel: these same brethren are found “in the very first chapter of the Acts of the Apostles joining with the Apostles… in prayer” (Acts 1:14), “a striking instance of the hardened ground… passing into the fruitful ground,” the cause of the change being the risen Lord’s appearance to James (1 Corinthians 15:7).

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the doctrine of grace. The verse shows “how small is the value of carnal relationship,” the Spirit stamping “with a perpetual mark of infamy the relations of Christ, because, though convinced by the testimonies of so many works, they did not even then believe.” Only the new creature counts (2 Corinthians 5:17; Galatians 6:15): “they who dedicate themselves wholly to God obtain the place of father, and mother, and brethren to Christ” (Matthew 12:50). Gill agrees that kinship after the flesh profits nothing without “the same grace, or the same faith.” Calvin turns the point polemically against the veneration of Mary “only on the ground of relationship,” recalling Christ’s own answer to the woman who blessed the womb that bore him — “Nay, rather, blessed are they who hear the word of God” (Luke 11:27–28).

Barnes points the warning home (citing Mark 3:21): nearness to Christ in the flesh is no substitute for faith, and the unbelief of his own household stands as a sober witness that the gospel divides even families until grace prevails. The very brethren who here mock will, after the resurrection, be numbered among the believers — but only when they cease to be merely his kinsmen and become his disciples.
6:
Christ’s reply — “My time is not yet come; but your time is alway ready” — turns the witnesses to the meaning of his “time.” The grammatical-historical commentators distinguish the word carefully. Robertson notes it is kairos, not the hōra John usually uses for “the predestined hour,” but “the fitting or proper occasion for Christ’s manifesting himself publicly to the authorities as Messiah,” as he will at this very feast (vv.14, 28). Ellicott reads it of “events of life… marked out by divinely-ordered seasons”: there is a time for Christ to enter Jerusalem “with a throng of pilgrims,” but it is not now. Barnes guesses Jesus avoided going up with the crowds lest it “have too much the appearance of parade and ostentation” and excite “the envy and opposition of the rulers,” while “your time is alway ready” means the brothers’ going “will excite no tumult.” Clarke prefers the simplest sense — “he had some business to transact before he could go” — over the reading that takes “my time” of his passion.

Calvin, for the Reformed, agrees the “time” here “denotes the time of his setting out on the journey,” not of his death, and presses the contrast: the brothers “may freely and without danger appear, at all hours, before the world, because the world is friendly and favourable to them; but he is in dread of his person… because the world is his mortal enemy.” By the saying, Calvin adds, Christ means “that they do wrong in giving advice on a matter which they do not understand.” The verse thus sets the believer’s deepest division from the world in the simplest of terms: the man whose times are governed by the Father’s purpose cannot move at the world’s convenience, for the world that welcomes the unbelieving brother seeks the life of the Son.

Robertson observes that at this very feast Jesus would in fact make his public claims openly (John 7:29, 33; 8:28), so the refusal is not of disclosure but of disclosure on the brothers’ terms and timetable. The lesson the commentators draw is that the believer’s seasons are not his own to fix: what is “alway ready” for those at ease in the world waits, for the servant of God, upon a purpose he did not set and may not hurry.
7:
“The world cannot hate you; but me it hateth, because I testify of it, that the works thereof are evil.” The witnesses find here the root of the world’s enmity to Christ. The grammatical-historical commentators read the contrast as one of kind. Barnes: the brothers “profess no principles in opposition to the world… you possess the same spirit and principles with the men of the world,” so “they cannot be expected to hate you”; but Christ is hated because “he proclaimed that men were depraved,” and “all who preach faithfully against the wickedness of men will excite opposition.” Clarke takes “the world” here of “the Jewish people only,” who will not persecute the brothers because they share its hope of “a worldly Messiah,” while they hate Christ “because I condemn its injustice, its pride, its ambition.” Robertson appeals to Westcott’s “law of moral correspondence”: the unbelieving world “is unable to love Jesus,” and resents the exposure (John 3:19; John 15:18). Ellicott deepens it: “men who love darkness must also hate light,” for the light’s “very presence makes the darkness visible.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the saying a verdict on fallen nature. Peace with the world “can only be purchased by a wicked consent to vices,” and “the world” denotes “men who are not born again, who retain their natural disposition”; thus “all who have not yet been regenerated by the Spirit are Christ’s adversaries.” The reason is that he condemns the world, so that “the Gospel cannot be faithfully preached without summoning the whole world, as guilty, to the judgment-seat of God” — the work of the Spirit who “will reprove the world of sin” (John 16:8). Wesley sums it briefly: the hatred falls on Christ “and all that bear the same testimony.” The witnesses thus turn the brothers’ taunt back on them: their very safety in the world is the proof that they belong to it.

The witnesses press the application. Barnes warns that “all who preach faithfully against the wickedness of men will excite opposition,” and that this “is not to deter us from doing our duty, after the example of Jesus.” The hatred of the world, then, is no sign of failure but the predictable answer of darkness to the light; and the brothers’ exemption from it is the surest evidence of where they stand.
8:
“Go ye up unto this feast: I go not up yet unto this feast.” The verse is famous for an old charge, and the grammatical-historical commentators meet it head-on. Porphyry, Clarke recalls, “accuses our blessed Lord of falsehood, because he said here, I will not go… and yet afterwards he went.” The answer turns partly on the text and partly on the sense. On the text, Robertson judges that ouk (“I go not up”) is probably the true reading, not the smoother oupō (“not yet”), and that it is “not really contradictory” of v.10, since “the manner of Christ’s going up is precisely the opposite of the advice of the brothers.” On the sense, Clarke notes Jesus did not say “I will not go” but “I go not yet,” having “other business” first; Ellicott likewise: he is “not going up unto the feast in the sense in which they intended — openly, with the usual caravan from Galilee,” the present tense not excluding “a going up afterwards.” Barnes adds the motive of prudence: he stayed till about the middle of the feast (v.14), “until the mass of the people had gone up, so that his going might excite no attention.”

Lightfoot fits the moment into the larger story, relating it to Luke 9:51 — “when the time was come that he should be received up, he steadfastly set his face to go to Jerusalem” — for now, “when he had not above six months to live,” Christ “determines resolutely to give all due manifestations of himself,” sending out the seventy before his face (Luke 10:1). Gill, reading pastorally, hears in “go ye up” a warrant that “the ceremonial law was not yet abolished,” and that even “unregenerate persons ought to attend on the outward means… for it may be God may make use of it, for the enlightening of their minds.” The witnesses are thus united: rightly read, the words declare not a change of mind but a different manner and time of going — secret, not paraded — fixed by the Father’s purpose, not the brothers’ urging.
9:
“When he had said these words unto them, he abode still in Galilee.” The verse is brief — Robertson notes the constative aorist emeinen covers “a period of some days,” and Ellicott reckons Jesus “could not… have remained behind them more than three or four days” before appearing in Jerusalem (v.14) — but the Eastern fathers, reading on into the manner of his going “as it were in secret,” draw from it the chapter’s great lesson on Christ’s example. Theophylact distinguishes the two movements: at first “He will not go up… in order not to expose Himself to the rage of the Jews,” and afterwards “He goes up, not to suffer, but to teach.” He goes privately, Chrysostom explains, because although “He could have gone openly, and kept the violence… of the Jews in check, as He had often done before,” to do so “every time would have disclosed His divinity,” whereas “he wished to establish the fact of His incarnation, and to teach us the way of life.”

The point, for Chrysostom, is pedagogical: “the things done by Christ after the manner of men are not so done only to establish the Incarnation, but also to educate us for virtue.” Had he done all as God, “how could we have known, on falling in with such things as we wished not, what we must do?” So by going up secretly he teaches “us what we ought to do, who cannot check our persecutors.” And the Evangelist says not “in secret” but “as it were in secret,” Chrysostom and Theophylact both note, “to show that it was done as a kind of economy.” Augustine adds that he went up “not to get temporary glory, but to teach wholesome doctrine, and remind men of the eternal feast.” Calvin, for the Reformed, closes on the warning beneath the contrast: the cousins, “in compliance with ordinary customs, pretend to worship God,” yet are “on friendly terms with unbelievers,” while Christ delays until his office compels him — and “accursed be that peace which costs so high a price as to leave and abandon the Son of God.”
10:
“But when his brethren were gone up, then went he also up unto the feast, not openly, but as it were in secret.” The grammatical-historical commentators read the verse as Christ’s deliberate rejection of his brothers’ counsel. Robertson notes the pointed verbal echo: they had urged him to “manifest” himself (phanerōson, v.4) “openly” (en phanerō), and he goes up “not openly” (ou phanerōs) “but as it were in secret” (en kryptō) — “plainly Jesus purposely went contrary to the insincere counsel of his brothers as to the manner of his Messianic manifestation.” Both Robertson and Bernard stress that “this secrecy concerned solely the journey to Jerusalem, not his public teaching there after his arrival” (vv.26, 28; 18:20): he travelled apart from the festal caravan, perhaps by the Samaritan road of John 4:4.

Ellicott presses a finer textual point with theological weight. The right reading joins “unto the feast” to the brothers’ going — “when His brethren were gone up unto the feast, then went He also up” — so that, strictly, “it is really nowhere said that He went up to the feast at all” in the public, festal sense the brothers meant; he appears only at “the Middle of the Feast” (v.14), the lesser days from the sixteenth to the twentieth of Tishri. Clarke adds the plain occasion — “having despatched his business, and the concourse of people being now past, he went up also” — and Wesley marks the solemn place of the journey in the story: “this was his last journey but one to Jerusalem; the next time he went up he suffered.” The witnesses thus agree that the manner of the going is the message: Christ will not be hurried into a worldly display, but orders his own manifestation by the Father’s timing and not the world’s.

The irony is not lost on the witnesses: the brothers had demanded a spectacle, and Christ answers with hiddenness on the road — yet the teaching he will give once arrived (v.14) is bolder and more public than any parade they imagined. The contrast fixes the chapter’s lesson that the manner and hour of self-disclosure belong to him alone, and are governed by the Father’s purpose rather than the impatience of unbelief.
11:
“Then the Jews sought him at the feast, and said, Where is he?” The witnesses are agreed on who “the Jews” are and what their seeking means. The grammatical-historical commentators distinguish them sharply from the crowds: by “the Jews” is meant “the official representatives of the nation” (Ellicott), “the hostile leaders in Jerusalem, not the Galilean crowds” nor “the populace” (Robertson), the “scribes, Pharisees, and rulers of the people” (Clarke). The imperfect “were seeking” (ezētoun) pictures their settled watch for a man who “had not yet appeared in public at the feast” and who “had avoided Jerusalem since the collision in chapter 5.” Their question — “Where is he?”, with the contemptuous emphatic ekeinos, almost “Where is that fellow?” (Ellicott, Robertson) — betrays, the commentators agree, that “their hostility had gone as far as a definite plot against Him, and that the knowledge of this was widely spread.” The leaders, Robertson adds, “had kept in touch with Christ’s work in Galilee” and “anticipate a crisis in Jerusalem.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, lifts the scene into a portrait of the church in bondage. The Jews “gaped for the promised redemption like hungry men,” yet “when Christ appears to them, they remain in suspense,” and from this arose “that murmuring and that variety of opinions.” That the people “whisper secretly is an indication of the tyranny which the priests and scribes exercised over them.” It is, he says, “a shocking exhibition” — “this Church, which was at that time the only Church on earth, is here represented to us as a confused and shapeless chaos,” the rulers holding the people “oppressed by fear and terror,” with “shameful desolation and lamentable disorder” throughout. So the leaders’ hunt for Christ becomes a window on a flock ruled by terror rather than fed by pastors — the very disorder the coming verses will display.

Gill notes that the seekers were of two kinds — “some to take him and kill him, and others to hear his doctrine” — for “all expected him at the feast, knowing it was always his custom” to attend (compare John 11:56, where the same anxious search recurs at a later feast). The very absence of Christ from the early days thus heightens the suspense, the leaders watching for a man whose every public appearance now carries the weight of a coming crisis.
12:
“And there was much murmuring among the people concerning him.” The witnesses dwell on the word and on the split it reveals. The grammatical-historical commentators note that “people” here is, uniquely in John, plural — “the multitudes” (Ellicott, Robertson) — the mixed throngs of Galileans and Judeans gathered for the feast, already divided in mind as the Galileans had been after the synagogue discourse (John 6:66). Robertson calls gongysmos an “onomatopoetic word” for “secret displeasure” (compare Acts 6:1; Philippians 2:14). The “practical test-question” (Ellicott) concerns not his teaching but his character: some said “He is a good man” — agathos, “pure in motive,” Robertson notes, a stronger word than “just” — while others said, “Nay; but he deceiveth the people,” the verb planaō (to lead astray) anticipating the rulers’ final epithet, “that deceiver” (Matthew 27:63). Clarke observes the inequality of the debate: the slanderers “spoke out, because they had the rulers on their side,” while those who spoke well “were obliged to do it in private,” for “calumny and slander are among the privileged orders.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, turns the division into counsel for the believer. “The diversity of opinion… proves that it is not a new evil, that men should differ in their opinions about Christ, even in the very bosom of the Church”; as we receive the Christ “formerly condemned by the greater part of his own nation,” so “we ought to be armed with the same kind of shield, that the dissensions which we see daily may not disturb us.” He marks too “how great is the rashness of men in the things of God,” who hasten to judge “the Son of God and… his most holy doctrine” where in a trifle they would be more cautious — and he calls for “the greater moderation,” lest we “thoughtlessly condemn” what we have not weighed.

Wesley adds that the murmuring was “much whispering, many private debates with each other, among those who were come from distant parts.” The witnesses agree the striking thing is that the debate turns on his character rather than his doctrine — is he sincere or a fraud? — so that the crowd, lacking the courage to decide, divides into hushed and opposing camps, each held back from open speech by the shadow of the rulers.
13:
“Howbeit no man spake openly of him for fear of the Jews.” The witnesses read this as the measure of the rulers’ tyranny. The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the words: Robertson notes the imperfect “was speaking” (elalei) picturing the whispered talk, the adverbial parrēsia (“openly, boldly”), and the objective genitive “for fear of the Jews” — the same dread that later silenced the disciples (John 19:38; John 20:19). Barnes takes “openly” as “boldly,” and finds here Christ’s timid friends: many “supposed that he was the Messiah, yet even this they did not dare to profess,” and the most they would venture was that “he was a good man.” He turns it on the reader — there are “always many such friends of Jesus… desirous of saying something good about him,” who “from fear or shame” will praise “his morals, his precepts, and his holy life, while they are ashamed to speak of his divinity or his atonement.” Ellicott widens the point: the silence “cannot fairly be limited… to the multitude who believed in Him,” but “discloses to us rather a reign of terror, in which opinion was stifled, and men dared not speak openly on either side until authority had determined what they should say.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, names the rulers’ aim. They “burned with such hatred against Christ, that they did not permit a word to be uttered on either side,” desiring above all “that his name should be buried in oblivion” — the last resort of “the enemies of the truth, after having found that they gain nothing by their cruelty.” That all were “subdued by fear” he calls “a proof of gross tyranny”; yet, he adds, “the power of our Lord Jesus Christ shone forth with greater and more wonderful brightness” when, despite it, “he caused himself to be heard amidst armed foes.” The hush of fear is thus the dark backdrop against which Christ’s open teaching at the feast (v.14) will blaze the brighter.

The contrast the witnesses draw is pointed: the slander of Christ’s enemies “stalked abroad,” as Clarke put it, while the good that might be said of him could only be “a whisper in secret.” That asymmetry — calumny loud and protected, witness timid and afraid — is itself the indictment of a leadership that ruled the conscience by fear, and the foil for the fearless voice about to be raised in the temple.
14:
The witnesses mark the turning of the chapter: at mid-feast Christ appears suddenly in the temple and teaches. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the moment — the Chol Moʿed or “Middle of the Feast,” the lesser days (Ellicott, Lightfoot) — and note this is, so far as we know, his first public appearance “as a public teacher,” probably expounding some Scripture that had been read (Ellicott; cf. v.19). Robertson parses the genitive absolute (“the feast being already midway”) and the inchoative imperfect “began to teach,” adding the dramatic stroke: the leaders had asked where he was (v.11), and “there he was now before their very eyes.”

The Eastern fathers read the timing as design. Chrysostom says Christ “delays His visit, in order to excite men’s attention,” so that those who had searched for him, seeing him “thus suddenly appear, would be more attentive to His teaching, both favourers and enemies.” Theophylact adds that at the feast’s start men were absorbed in its own ceremonies and “afterwards would be better disposed to hear Christ.” Augustine, taking up the crowd’s charge that “he seduceth the people” (v.12), turns it into a meditation: “to seduce” is to lead from one thing to another, so all turns on “the whence and the whither” — “if from evil to good, the seducer is a good man,” and in that sense “would that all of us” might so lead and be led.

Calvin, for the Reformed, and Henry, for the Puritans, draw the practical and the doctrinal. Calvin notes Christ “was not so much afraid as to desist from the execution of his office,” teaching that “we may sometimes expose ourselves to dangers, but we ought never to disregard or omit a single opportunity of doing good.” On his teaching at the feast he observes that God never meant the ceremonies to be “cold and useless spectacles”: “external rites are lively images of spiritual things, when they take their shape from the word of God,” so that, the priests being “dumb” and the doctrine corrupted by the scribes, “Christ undertook the office of a teacher.” Henry asks why he did not preach sooner, and answers that the people now “would have more leisure to hear him, and… be better disposed,” the gospel being “the same to the plain and to the polite.”
15:
“And the Jews marvelled, saying, How knoweth this man letters, having never learned?” The witnesses agree the wonder is real but ill-directed. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that “letters” (grammata) means here the learning of the schools — the Scriptures and traditions expounded in the rabbinic academies (Barnes, Clarke). Robertson traces the word from “the letters formed” to “the sacred Scriptures” to “learning” (compare Acts 26:24; 2 Timothy 3:15), quoting Westcott: “the marvel was that Jesus showed Himself familiar with the literary methods of the time, which were supposed to be confined to the scholars of the popular teachers.” Ellicott sharpens the point: “it is not the substance of His teaching that excites their attention, but the fact that He who has never been technically trained as a Rabbi is acquainted with the literature of the schools” — to them he is “a layman and unlearned” (compare Acts 4:13), a demagogue unlocking “mysteries of which they thought that they only possessed the key.” Marcus Dods, Robertson notes, fixes the precise marvel: “it is not the wisdom of Jesus that disconcerted the Jewish leaders, but his learning.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, turns their astonishment into a mirror of human perversity. Their admiration is “of such a nature, that they seek occasion from it to despise him,” for “in judging of the works of God, they always seek deliberately an occasion of falling into error.” When God works by ordinary means, those means are “as it were veils which hinder us from perceiving the Divine hand”; but when “an unwonted power of God shines above the order of nature… we are stunned,” and yet, “such is our pride,” what ought to move us “passes away as a dream,” because “we take no interest in any thing of which we do not know the reason.” That Christ, “untaught,” should so handle the Scriptures was, Calvin says, “an astonishing proof of the power and grace of God” — the very proof they let slip.

Gill adds that they marvelled at both “the matter, and manner of his doctrine,” his words so “gracious” and full of truth that “they were astonished”; and Wesley fixes the precise wonder — how, “never having learned at any place of education,” he could “expound the Scripture with such propriety and gracefulness.” The astonishment, the witnesses agree, stood on the very threshold of faith, yet went no further than amazement, because admiration without submission cannot recognise the hand of God.
16:
Christ’s answer — “My doctrine is not mine, but his that sent me” — meets the wonder of v.15 by tracing his teaching to its source. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the claim of origin. Barnes: “it is not originated by me… I teach nothing that is contrary to the will of God,” so “the doctrine is divine in its origin and in its nature.” Robertson notes the recurring Johannine idiom “the one who sent me” (John 5:30; 6:38) and finds here “the bold claim… that his teaching is superior in character and source to that of the rabbis,” the New Testament set against the Talmud. Clarke reads it as the language of an ambassador — “I speak not my own words, but his who sent me” — drawing the hearers “from the teaching of man to the teaching of God,” and identifying Jesus as the Messiah on whom “the Spirit of Jehovah” rests (Isaiah 11:2). Ellicott adds that he is “in His humanity as a messenger, carrying the message of Him that sent Him… the Word by whom the mind of God is spoken.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the verse turn the offence into evidence. The very circumstance that stumbled them “was rather a ladder by which they ought to have risen higher”: “when you see a teacher not trained in the school of men, know that I have been taught by God.” God brought his Son “out of a mechanic’s workshop, rather than from the schools of the scribes,” precisely “that the origin of the Gospel might be more manifest,” that none “might imagine that any human being was the author of it” — just as he later chose “ignorant and uneducated men to be his apostles,” instructing them “in a single instant” so they came forth “as new men.” Wesley sums it: the doctrine is “not acquired by any labour of learning, but his that sent me — immediately infused by him.”

The witnesses draw the abiding lesson that all true teaching in the church derives its authority not from the teacher’s genius but from God whose message it bears. The minister, like the Master, is an ambassador; the doctrine is owned not because it is his but because it is the Father’s who sent him. So the answer both humbles the teacher and exalts the word, and turns the hearers from wondering at the man to weighing the message — which is the very thing the next verse will make the test of all true doctrine.
17:
This verse — “If any man will do his will, he shall know of the doctrine, whether it be of God” — is read by all the witnesses as Christ’s test of his own teaching, grounding spiritual knowledge in moral willingness. The grammatical-historical commentators press the emphatic verb. Robertson and Ellicott insist the stress is on “willeth”: not deed, which is faith’s outcome, but “will, which precedes it” — a third-class condition with the full force of thelō (Robertson), so that “this human will to do the divine will is the condition of knowing it.” The knowing is “experimental,” resting on “moral harmony between man’s purpose and God’s will”; quoting Westcott, “if there be no sympathy there can be no understanding,” so that “atheists of all types have no point of contact for approach to the knowledge of Christ,” being “out of tune with the Infinite.” Barnes describes the resulting evidence as “internal… satisfactory and conclusive,” of two kinds: the doctrines “commend themselves to his reason and conscience,” and the heart that tries them finds them “fitted to make us pure and happy.” Clarke makes it a plain rule: “begin the practice” of God’s will, apply Christ’s precepts, and if one is found “contrary to the nature, perfections, and glory of God,” reject it; if not, “acknowledge that God has visited you.”

Ellicott widens the saying past its first hearers, who would take “his will” of the Law and Prophets (v.19), to “the will of God revealed, more or less clearly, to all men and in all times” — Saul the Pharisee, Cornelius the centurion, Justin the philosopher — for “the ‘any man’ of Christ’s own words excludes none.” Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the same point as a rebuke: “sound judgment flows from fear and reverence for God,” and the reason the hearers “cannot distinguish between falsehood and truth” is that “they want the principal requisite to sound understanding, namely, piety, and the earnest desire to obey God.” Wesley calls it “a universal rule, with regard to all persons and doctrines”: “he that is thoroughly willing to do it, shall certainly know what the will of God is.” The verse thus answers the marvel of v.15 at a deeper level — the obstacle to recognising Christ’s teaching is not want of learning but want of a heart bent to obey.
18:
“He that speaketh of himself seeketh his own glory: but he that seeketh his glory that sent him, the same is true, and no unrighteousness is in him.” The witnesses take this as Christ’s second test — now of the teacher, by the glory he seeks. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the contrast. To speak “of himself” (aph’ heautou) is to speak “by his own authority, without being sent by God” (Barnes), the self-taught man who “pushes his own ideas, presses his own claims for position and glory” (Robertson). Such a one seeks his own praise; but Christ “manifestly sought to honour God,” and so should have been owned as sent by him. On the closing phrase several insist that “no unrighteousness” means here not sinlessness in the abstract but “no falsehood, no deception” — “he is not an impostor” (Barnes); Clarke notes that adikia often renders the Hebrew sheker, “a lie” (citing Psalm 52:3; Psalm 119:163), and Robertson sets it against “true” (alēthēs), as deceit opposed to truth (2 Thessalonians 2:10; 1 Corinthians 13:6). Ellicott connects it back to the murmured charge that he “deceiveth the people” (v.12): the professional teachers, who “sought glory one from another” (John 5:44), “in the pride of their own knowledge willed not the glory of God,” and so lacked the faculty to know his teaching.

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the verse a standing touchstone: “every thing that displays the glory of God is holy and divine; but every thing that contributes to the ambition of men… ought to be vehemently rejected.” He who “shall try and prove by this touchstone what is brought forward in the name of God will never be deceived,” and “no man can faithfully discharge the office of teacher in the Church, unless he be void of ambition.” The Alexandrian Origen, reflecting on the same “no unrighteousness,” reasons toward Christ as righteousness himself, since “there is no unrighteousness with God,” a “righteous and holy Lord” who “orders all things righteously.” Wesley glosses it simply, “no deceit or falsehood.” The two tests of vv.17–18 thus stand together — a willing heart knows true doctrine, and a God-glorifying aim marks the true teacher.
19:
“Did not Moses give you the law, and yet none of you keepeth the law? Why go ye about to kill me?” The witnesses read this as Christ’s sudden turn from defence to indictment. The Eastern Chrysostom frames the logic: the Jews brought “two charges against Christ; one, that He broke the sabbath; the other, that He said God was His Father.” Having answered the second, Christ meets the first — “the law says, You shall not kill, whereas you kill” — so that “if I broke a law to heal a man, it was a transgression, but a beneficial one; whereas you transgress for an evil end.” The grammatical-historical commentators stress the force of the question. Robertson reads the kai as adversative — “and yet they violated that law by not practising it” — and hears a deliberate allusion to the earlier attempt on his life (John 5:18), the very words recurring. Lightfoot locates “the emphasis… chiefly in the word me”: “none of you all perform the law as you ought; and yet your great design is to kill me… why me, and not others?” Barnes notes the irony that those who “prided themselves” on the law, counting “every violation… deserving of death,” were themselves “its violators in seeking his life” (Romans 3:10). Ellicott adds the seasonal fitness: Moses had commanded the Law be read at this very feast in a Sabbatical year (Deuteronomy 31:10).

Calvin, for the Reformed, observes that Christ “reasons, not from the subject, but from the person”: since they “freely indulged in their vices, as if they had never known any law,” their professed “zeal for the Law” in hating him is exposed as hypocrisy. The Latin Augustine lifts the scene to its height — “both men of truth and liars heard the Truth speaking; both friends and enemies heard Charity speaking” — and Christ “saw and foresaw whom His discourse profited,” a comfort to us “who have not seen Him, and yet have believed.” The murderous intent the crowd will deny (v.20) is thus named openly by the One who reads every heart.
20:
“The people answered and said, Thou hast a devil: who goeth about to kill thee?” The witnesses agree this is the voice of the ignorant pilgrim crowd, not the plotting rulers. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the speakers as “the Galilean crowd” and other festival pilgrims, “unfamiliar with the effort to kill Jesus recorded in John 5:18” (Robertson, who carefully distinguishes the chapter’s several groups — leaders, Galilean multitude, Jerusalemites, soldiers). “Thou hast a devil,” Barnes explains, means “thou art deranged or mad” (compare John 10:20); seeing “no effort to kill him,” and ignorant of the rulers’ design, they took his words for “the effect of derangement.” Ellicott softens the tone: they speak “not in hostility, but in wonder that He can think so,” supposing such a thought “must be the work of a demon.” Robertson cites Dods’ blunt paraphrase of their view — “He is a monomaniac labouring under a hallucination that people wish to kill him.” Clarke notes a textual variant by which some manuscripts assign the retort to “the Jews,” which would show their malice the more, though the better reading keeps it with the crowd.

Calvin, for the Reformed, explains the idiom: among the Jews, to say one “has a devil” was to say “thou art mad,” since they held that men “excited to rage, or… who have lost sense and reason, are tormented by the devil” — God sometimes employing “the devil as the executioner and minister of his wrath.” The multitude, “not acquainted with the intentions of the priests,” foolishly “ascribe it to madness” when Christ complains they seek his life. From this Calvin draws a caution “that we ought to be exceedingly cautious not to form an opinion about subjects” we do not understand — the crowd condemning as delusion what was sober truth, and slandering the Lord out of mere ignorance.

Gill and Wesley add that these were “country people” from Galilee and distant parts, “not his downright enemies… but rather seemed to favour him,” yet “greatly provoked to hear him talk after this manner.” The very vehemence of their denial — “Who goeth about to kill thee?” — becomes, in the witnesses’ reading, an unwitting measure of how secret the rulers had kept their murderous design, and how truly Christ, in naming it, spoke from a knowledge the crowd did not share.
21:
“I have done one work, and ye all marvel.” The witnesses take this as Christ resuming, undeterred by the charge of madness, the defence of his Sabbath healing. The grammatical-historical commentators identify “one work” as the cure of the impotent man at the pool (John 5:1ff.) on his last visit to Jerusalem — the single deed that, Robertson notes, “caused the rulers to try to kill Jesus” (John 5:18), though he had wrought other works before (John 2:23; 4:45). The marvel, Barnes and Ellicott agree, was “that he should dare to do what they esteemed a violation of the Sabbath”; this “work on the Sabbath day, which provoked the deadly hostility of the hierarchy,” was “cause of wonder to them all,” drawing even the well-meaning crowd toward a hostile posture. Clarke and others note the disputed punctuation, by which the “therefore” (dia touto) of the next verse may better join here — “ye all marvel because of this” — a reading Clarke says “all the eminent critics” favour.

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the shift in the argument: “leaving their persons, he begins to speak of the fact,” proving “that the miracle which he performed is not inconsistent with the Law of God.” The point of “one work” is that “it is only of a single crime that he is held guilty,” a single Sabbath cure, “but that they, on every day of Rest, do many works of the same… and do not reckon them criminal” — for “not a day of Rest passed on which there were not many infants in Judea” to be circumcised. Wesley sums it: “one work, out of many… and ye all marvelled at it, because I did it on the Sabbath day.” The defence by circumcision is thus set up, to be pressed home in the verses that follow.

The rhetorical strategy, the witnesses observe, is deliberate: by naming a single, undeniable work and the marvel it caused, Christ fixes their attention on the one act they condemned, the better to turn their own law and custom against their verdict. He does not multiply instances but isolates the one, so that the inconsistency he is about to expose cannot be lost in a crowd of examples.
22:
“Moses therefore gave unto you circumcision (not because it is of Moses, but of the fathers); and ye on the sabbath day circumcise a man.” The witnesses unfold both the parenthesis and the argument. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the qualifying clause: Moses “commanded you to circumcise your children” (Leviticus 12:3), but the rite was “not because it is of Moses” — he “found it already in existence, and incorporated it in his institutions” — being “of the fathers,” given to Abraham (Genesis 17:12; Genesis 21:4; Barnes, Ellicott). On the practice itself, Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic rule that decides the case: though ordinary work forbidden on the Sabbath must be done beforehand, “circumcision, when it cannot be done on the vespers of the sabbath, may be done on the sabbath day,” for “circumcision… nulleth the sabbath.” The eighth day after birth could fall on a Sabbath, and then, by the teaching of the school of Hillel that prevailed at Jerusalem (Ellicott), the circumcision was performed regardless, the lesser law of the Sabbath yielding to the law of the covenant sign.

The argument Christ builds on this, the witnesses agree, is one his hearers could not deny from their own practice: they themselves set aside the Sabbath rest to circumcise a single member of the body. Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that the comparison is not merely of like with like but prepares a movement “from the greater to the less” — if the partial work of circumcision rightly honours the Sabbath, how much more the whole healing of a man. The parenthesis about “the fathers,” several add, quietly reminds them that the covenant runs back behind Moses to Abraham, so that the rite they keep on the Sabbath is itself older and deeper than the technical Sabbath rule they invoke against Christ.

Lightfoot adds the rabbinic debate over circumcising even a proselyte’s child on the Sabbath, showing how finely the schools weighed when the rite might override the day; and the witnesses agree the force of Christ’s appeal lies in turning that very casuistry against its keepers. They had a rule by which one law rightly yielded to another; let them apply the same principle, and they must concede that mercy to a whole man may rightly be shown on the Sabbath too.
23:
“If a man on the sabbath day receive circumcision, that the law of Moses should not be broken; are ye angry at me, because I have made a man every whit whole on the sabbath day?” The witnesses read this as the climax of Christ’s Sabbath defence, an argument from the lesser to the greater. The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the comparison. Barnes states it plainly: “you yourselves… allow a work of necessity to be done” — circumcision, “an ordinance of religion denoting separation from other nations, or external purity” — and “you ought also, for the same reason, to allow that a man should be completely restored to health,” a work “of much more importance.” Clarke heightens the contrast: circumcision removed only “a small portion of flesh,” a mark of reproach, whereas Christ opposes to it “the complete cure of the infirm man, who was diseased throughout his whole body.” Robertson dwells on the phrase “a man every whit whole” (holon anthrōpon hygiē) — “a whole man… sound, not just one member of the body mended” — and on the vivid verb “are ye wroth” (cholate, from the word for bile), “only here in the New Testament,” a “picture of bitter spleen.” On the textual question Ellicott prefers the reading by which “the law… not broken” refers to the law of circumcision on the eighth day, which the Sabbath yields to.

Calvin, for the Reformed, fixes the form of the reasoning. There is a “similarity between… the cure of the paralytic” and circumcision — “both were works of God” — but Christ “draws a comparison between the greater and the less.” If the Jews allow that circumcision, performed on the Sabbath, does not violate the Law, because the two “agree well with each other,” they cannot consistently condemn a far greater work of God done on the same day. Their anger, the witnesses agree, is therefore not zeal for the Law but, as the next verse will charge, the fruit of “a prejudice which they have formed against his person.”

The witnesses thus see the argument close like a trap of the hearers’ own making: every Sabbath circumcision they perform is a standing concession that the day bends to a higher good. To rage at a healing while honouring a cutting is to strain at a gnat and swallow a camel — and the inconsistency, Calvin says, can only be explained by a heart already set against the person of Christ.
24:
“Judge not according to the appearance, but judge righteous judgment.” The witnesses take this as the moral of the whole Sabbath argument, a call to weigh deeds by their substance rather than their surface. The grammatical-historical commentators expound the contrast. Barnes: judge “not as a thing first offers itself to you, without reflection or candour” — in appearance the Sabbath cure “might be a violation of the law,” as circumcision might seem to be, “yet it is right to do works of necessity and mercy.” Clarke deepens it: “the law which commands men to rest on the Sabbath day is subordinate to the law of mercy and love,” and one should judge “not from the first impression… but from its tendency, and the motives of the person… still believing the best, where you have no certain proof to the contrary.” Robertson glosses “according to appearance” (kat’ opsin) as “superficially.” Ellicott adds a pointed reminder: the command to “judge righteous judgment” is itself “another Positive Precept of Moses which these judges were forgetting” (Deuteronomy 1:16–17), part of the very section of the Law read at Tabernacles, so that even in this Christ holds them to their own Scripture.

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes it a rebuke of prejudice. They are “carried away by wicked dispositions, and do not form judgment according to the fact and the matter in hand.” They rightly hold that circumcision on the Sabbath does not violate the Law, “but why do they not arrive at the same conclusion as to the work of Christ, but because their minds are preoccupied by a prejudice which they have formed against his person?” Judgment, he says, “will never be right unless it be regulated by the truth of the fact,” for “as soon as persons appear in public, they turn their eyes and senses on them, so that the truth immediately vanishes.” The verse thus gathers the chapter’s repeated lesson: that the failure to know Christ rightly is, at bottom, a failure to judge righteously — a heart that prefers its prejudice to the plain work of God before its eyes.
25:
“Then said some of them of Jerusalem, Is not this he, whom they seek to kill?” The witnesses fasten on the phrase “of Jerusalem.” The grammatical-historical commentators note these are the city’s own residents (Robertson’s rare word Hierosolymitōn), “distinct from the multitude” of pilgrims (v.20) and, unlike them, “acquainted with the intention” of the rulers (Ellicott); they “knew of their threats to kill Jesus” (John 5:18) and wonder that he now “speaketh boldly, and they say nothing unto him.” The Eastern Chrysostom presses why “of Jerusalem” is added: “they who had most enjoyed His mighty miracles were more pitiable than any,” having “beheld the greatest proof of His Godhead, and yet committed all to the judgment of their corrupt rulers”; that men “bent on murder… should be quiet of a sudden” was itself “a great proof” of his divine power.

The Latin Augustine reads the moment against the chapter’s opening: Christ had gone up “as it were in secret,” to signify “that even in that very feast… He Himself was hidden, and that the mystery of the feast was His own”; now “that which was supposed to be timidity appeared as power,” for “He spoke openly… so that the crowds marvelled.” Calvin, for the Reformed, adds that these Jerusalemites “have some reason for wondering” that the seething rulers let Christ preach freely, “but they err in this respect, that in a miracle altogether Divine they do not take into account the providence of God” — the very providence, Henry agrees, by which “God can tie men’s hands, though he does not turn their hearts.” The bystanders see the strange restraint of the rulers; they do not see the hand that restrains them.

Gill underscores why it is the city-dwellers who speak: “living in the city, they knew more of the temper and disposition, the designs and attempts, of the chief priests, Scribes, and elders, to take away the life of Christ.” Their question, then, is not idle but informed — they grasp the danger the pilgrims could not, and so feel the full strangeness of seeing the hunted man teach unmolested in the very temple. What to the Galileans looked like a madman’s fear is to these Jerusalemites a marvel of unaccountable restraint, which the witnesses trace, with Calvin and Henry, to the unseen providence of God.
26:
“But, lo, he speaketh boldly, and they say nothing unto him. Do the rulers know indeed that this is the very Christ?” The witnesses, all grammatical-historical here, read the Jerusalemites’ reasoning and its irony. Their inference, Barnes explains, runs thus: they “knew that they had attempted to kill him,” yet now “saw him speaking boldly to the people without interruption,” and so “concluded that some change had taken place in the sentiments of the rulers,” as though the Sanhedrin had been privately convinced and were merely slow to publish it. Robertson catches the tone: the rulers “say nothing unto him” but “only make sneering comments,” so the question carries “sarcasm about the leaders,” with perhaps “an element of surprise” in these “shrewd townsmen.” The grammar confirms it: the question is framed with mēpote, “expecting a negative answer” (Robertson, Ellicott) — “the questioners think it impossible that the rulers can have recognised Him” — and the verb is ingressive, “have the rulers come to know” (Ellicott’s “Have the rulers come to know indeed that this Man is the Christ?”).

On the words “the very Christ,” the commentators note a textual point: the second “very” (alēthōs) is wanting in the best manuscripts (Clarke marshals a long list of authorities, with Griesbach and Bishop Pearce, against it), so the question reads simply “that this is the Christ.” Either way the sense is plain — the bystanders float, half in sarcasm and half in genuine puzzlement, the impossible thought that the very rulers who sought his death might now secretly own his claim. Their question, the witnesses imply, is shrewder than they know: it raises, only to dismiss, the truth they themselves will not embrace — that this bold teacher, unhindered before his enemies, is indeed the Christ.

Clarke, following Calmet, notes that the crowd at this moment was “composed of three different classes”: the rulers and Pharisees, declared enemies; the inhabitants of Jerusalem, who “knew the secret” of the plot; and the strangers up for the feast, who did not. It is the middle group that speaks here, and their question betrays their own unbelief even as it exposes the rulers’ inconsistency — for they raise the possibility that the Sanhedrin has recognised the Christ only to treat it as plainly incredible. The witnesses find in this a portrait of a people watching the evidence accumulate and still refusing the conclusion it presses upon them.
27:
“Howbeit we know this man whence he is: but when Christ cometh, no man knoweth whence he is.” The witnesses explain the objection from a popular messianic expectation. The grammatical-historical commentators note the crowd’s confidence that they “know this man whence he is” — not chiefly his birthplace (which they wrongly took for Galilee) but “the manner of his birth,” reckoning him simply “the son of Joseph and Mary,” born “as other persons are” (Gill). Against this they set the saying that “when Christ cometh, no man knoweth whence he is.” Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background and resolves the apparent clash with v.42 and Matthew 2:5: the Jews “doubted not… but he should give the first manifestation of himself from Bethlehem; but then they supposed he would be hid again,” and “after some space of time make a new appearance, from what place no one could tell.” Jewish authors, he notes, told of a Messiah “born in Bethlehem, but immediately snatched away,” his origin thereafter unknown.

The irony, the witnesses agree, is double. The crowd’s very ground for rejecting Jesus — that his origin is too well known — rests on ignorance, for they know neither his true birthplace at Bethlehem nor, far more, his eternal origin from the Father, the “whence” that the whole Gospel has been unfolding (John 1:1; John 6:38). Calvin, for the Reformed, marks how their imagined knowledge becomes the very veil over their eyes: confident they have measured him, they will not look higher. Wesley notes they would not follow the rulers even should these believe, “because they knew… whence he was.” So the objection that sounds like knowledge is in truth a double blindness — to the prophecy they misread and to the Word made flesh they will not receive.

Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson agree that the popular maxim rested on a half-truth of prophecy stretched into error: Scripture did veil the Messiah’s deeper origin, but the people turned this into a flat denial that his earthly parentage could be known. The witnesses note the tragic symmetry with the Gospel’s whole argument — the crowd is sure of the one “whence” that does not finally matter, and blind to the “whence” that does. Their confident objection thus becomes, in John’s hands, an unwitting confession: they do not know where he is from, precisely because they will not see that he is from above.
28:
Jesus cries out in the temple, taking up the crowd's claim to know him — "ye both know me, and ye know whence I am" — and turning it against them: they know the surface, but not the Father who sent him. The witnesses divide over the tone of "ye know me."

The Reformed reading hears irony. Calvin: it is bitter reproach for their rashness — "you know all things, and yet you know nothing"; contrasting their false opinion with the truth, as if to say, "while your eyes are fixed on the earth you think every part of me is before you... but God will testify that I have come from heaven." He calls God "true" in the sense of 2 Timothy 2:13 — faithful, unable to deny himself — so that the credit of the gospel is not diminished though the whole world rise against it, citing the appeals of Jeremiah (Jeremiah 20:7), Isaiah (Isaiah 50:8), and Paul (1 Corinthians 4:5). Gill notes Christ "cried" — lifted his voice publicly, overhearing their reasonings.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the sense and the irony together. Ellicott: they knew his form and earthly home (v.27), "but all this was far short of the real knowledge of him"; "He that sent Me is true" means not merely truthful but "the ideally true One," the truly existent — "in him is my true origin, and him ye know not." Barnes reads the first clause as possibly a question; Robertson takes the kai as "and yet," Jesus tersely granting they know something of him while passing by the popular theology to expose "their ignorance of God the Father who sent him" (John 8:19, 55).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke takes Jesus up "on their own profession": add this to your knowledge — "I am not come of myself... I came from God," attested by the Baptist, the descending Spirit, the heavenly voice, the purity of the doctrine, and the multitude of miracles; "but ye know him not, therefore this testimony is disregarded" (John 5:43). Lightfoot adds the rabbinic colour: a man unknown to the priests might yet be credited for the purity of his offering, so "he that sent me, though you know him not, is worthy of belief." The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem sets the mission within the order of the Father's sending. Across the schools the verse turns a boast of knowledge into the proof of ignorance of God.
29:
"But I know him, for I am from him, and he hath sent me." Over against the crowd's ignorance the witnesses set Christ's unique knowledge of the Father — grounded, all agree, in his origin and mission, though they weigh how far it proves his eternal deity.

The grammatical-historical readers mark the emphatic pronouns and the claim. Ellicott: in contrast with their ignorance is "his own full knowledge, which belonged to One only" (John 1:18); he knows God "because he is from him, and in union ever one with him," and "in his human nature the representative of the Divine to mankind." Robertson calls "I know him" the "unique claim of Jesus, which is pressed all through the chapters of controversy" (8:55; 17:25; Matthew 11:27; Luke 10:22); the verb apesteilen ("he sent me") is the same used of sending the Twelve (Matthew 10:5) — "he is the Father's Apostle to men" (John 17:3).

The Reformed voice presses the warrant for boldness while guarding against overreach. Calvin: Christ's confidence is not lightly assumed, and "by his example he warns us not to assume lightly the name of God" as patron of our cause — a caution against "fanatics who give out their own inventions as the oracles of God"; yet when we have ascertained God's truth, we ought "boldly to resist men." He notes that some refer "I am from him" to Christ's divine essence and "he hath sent me" to his office; Calvin will not reject this, and grants his heavenly descent may be inferred, "but it would not be a sufficiently strong proof of his eternal Divinity against the Arians." Gill takes the knowledge as exhaustive — his nature, perfections, council and covenant — such as none has but he and those to whom he reveals it (Matthew 11:27).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley distributes the two clauses neatly: "I am from him — by eternal generation; and he hath sent me — his mission follows from his generation," answering exactly their two questions, "Do ye know me? Do ye know whence I am?" Across the schools, then, the verse is the chapter's sharpest claim of intimacy with the Father, the ground both of Christ's authority and of the believer's confidence — Calvin alone tempering how much dogmatic weight the bare words will bear.
30:
"Then they sought to take him; but no man laid hands on him, because his hour was not yet come." The witnesses read the failed arrest as a window onto divine providence governing the time of Christ's death — and, by extension, of every life.

The Reformed voice draws the doctrine out. Calvin: they had the will, made the attempt, and had the strength, yet were "benumbed, as if they had their hands and feet bound," because Christ was "guarded by the protection of God"; this also meets the offence of the cross, for Christ was dragged to death "not through the caprice of men, but because he was destined for such a sacrifice by the decree of the Father." Hence the general doctrine: "the time of every man's death has been fixed by God," and we are "safe from all risk until God is pleased to call us away" — yet without presumption, each following his own calling (Psalm 55:22; 1 Peter 5:7). Gill notes their rage at his claim to descend from God, whom they took for a mere carpenter's son.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the grammar and the theme. Robertson: the imperfect ezētoun ("began to seek"), and "his hour" — "John is at pains to point out at every point that the persecution and death of Jesus followed a predestined course" (2:4; 8:20; 13:1). Ellicott: the tense marks "a series of efforts"; the writer, "from the old age of his own life," has "learnt the religious interpretation of history," that every deed "had its hour mapped out in the eternal counsels of God" — though the immediate restraint was the division in the crowd (vv.43, 44), no one daring the decisive step. Barnes notes the rulers sought him for his reproof and his Messianic claim (Matthew 21:46).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley keeps it brief — "his hour: the time of his suffering" — but the schools converge: the arrest fails not by accident but because the appointed hour had not struck, the whole chapter (with v.32, 44) tracing the predestined course of the Passion to its set time.
31:
"Many of the people believed on him" and asked, "When the Christ cometh, will he do more miracles than these?" The witnesses read a real but imperfect faith, kindled by the signs, and weigh how far it reached.

The Eastern father is most exacting. Chrysostom (in the Catena): "neither had these a sound faith"; their "when Christ comes" shows "they were not steady in believing that he was the Christ," or did not believe it at all — "minds of the grosser sort are influenced not by doctrine, but by miracles." Augustine adds that the gospel "brought the poor and humble to be saved," who "received his medicine without hesitation," while the rulers, possessed with madness, sought to kill the physician.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers note the Messianic expectation behind the question. Barnes and Clarke ground it in Isaiah 35:5-6: the Messiah was expected to work many miracles, and miracles had ceased for centuries (even the Baptist wrought none), so Jesus' signs were "a full proof of his divine mission." Ellicott marks the advance from "some" calling him "a good man" (v.12) to "many" now believing: the Messianic idea is fulfilled, "and He who has fulfilled it must be the very Christ." Robertson reads the ingressive aorist — "many came to believe at this point."

The Reformed voice is gentlest with this small faith. Calvin: though they "depended more on miracles than they relied on doctrine," yet because they were "prepared to listen," such a preparation is honoured with the name of faith — "when the Holy Spirit bestows so honorable a designation on a small spark of good disposition, it ought to encourage us" that faith, however small, is acceptable to God. The Puritan Henry, soberly: "the discourses of Jesus convinced many that he was the Messiah, but they had not courage to own it." So the witnesses agree the faith was genuine seed, though weak — to be nourished, not despised.

The witnesses thus leave the verse poised between hope and warning: a genuine movement toward Christ, yet one resting on signs rather than on the word he had pressed them to weigh (v.17). Chrysostom's caution and Calvin's encouragement together frame the pastoral point — such faith is real seed to be nurtured, not a finished harvest, and it stands exposed to the contempt of the rulers who will shortly move to crush it (v.32).
32:
"The Pharisees heard the multitude murmuring... and the chief priests and the Pharisees sent officers to take him." The witnesses read the verse as the moment the leadership moves from suspicion to organized action, the rival parties uniting against Christ.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the politics. Robertson: the Pharisees, "particularly hostile," enlist the Sadducees (the chief priests) in the Sanhedrin — "the organized court" (7:45; 11:47) — to send hypēretas, the temple police (18:3; Acts 5:22); it was "the defence of Jesus by a portion of the crowd" (v.31) that irritated them, the murmuring now "grown louder like the hum of bees." Ellicott: the Pharisees take the first steps, see faith spreading, and "hastily call together the Sanhedrin," the chief priests, "for the most part Sadducees," joining in "an official resolve to take him by force."

The Reformed voice draws the lesson from their union. Calvin: the Pharisees, "like persons set on the watch," were anxious to keep Christ from being known; finding their own strength insufficient, they "committed the affair to the whole order of the priests," so that men who "differed among themselves now conspire together, under the guidance of Satan, against the Son of God." And he turns it on his readers: "since they had such ardent zeal... for defending their tyranny, how much more zealous ought we to be in maintaining the kingdom of Christ" — for the Papists in his day are "not less mad" to extinguish the gospel. Gill simply notes the people "murmured," privately whispering.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke reads the calculation: "the people began to be convinced that he was the Messiah," and the Pharisees "thought it high time to put him to death, lest the people should believe on him." So the schools agree: it is precisely the spread of faith that provokes the official machinery, the chapter's threat of arrest (v.30) now given a formal warrant.

The witnesses also note the ominous shift in agency: where before it was a vague "they sought" (v.30), now the formal court acts, issuing a warrant through its officers. Calvin's warning hangs over the scene — that the enemies of Christ were tireless in their zeal where his friends are often slack — and the chapter's irony begins to gather, for the very officers sent to silence him will return confessing that none ever spoke as he did (v.46).
33:
"Yet a little while am I with you, and then I go unto him that sent me." The witnesses read tender warning in the words, addressed (most agree) to his enemies even as they seek his life, with the first clear hint of his return to the Father.

The Wesleyan-Arminian and grammatical-historical readers stress the tenderness and the timing. Barnes: "this speech is full of tenderness" — to his persecutors he says he will not be long with them, implying they should "seek him while he was yet with them"; "enmity, and hate, and persecution did not prevent his offering salvation to them." Clarke dates it about six months before the cross, spoken so the well-disposed "might lay their hearts to his teaching while they had the privilege." Robertson: "I go unto him that sent me" (the same words in 16:5); hypagō, to withdraw, often in John of going to the Father — "enigmatic language to the hearers" (13:3; 14:28).

The Reformed voice hears Christ addressing his enemies directly. Calvin: he "ridicules their efforts, because they will be utterly ineffectual," his time being "decreed by the Father"; he reproaches their ingratitude — given to them from heavenly glory, "still there were few who received him" — and warns that God "will not long endure that his grace should be exposed to such shameful contempt." Neither his life nor his death is at their disposal; rather, after death he will be "declared to be the Son of God by the magnificent triumph of his resurrection." And generally: "the preaching of the Gospel is called Christ's descent to us" (Ephesians 2:17) — accept the hand he holds out, and he leads us to the Father; disregard him, and "he will leave us altogether strangers to God and to life."

Wesley notes the words simply continue the discourse interrupted at v.29, and Ellicott that the threat of arrest "brings to his mind the thought that the end is at hand," his mission to be "completed in its crowning sacrifice." Across the schools the verse joins mercy and warning: a short season of grace remains, and then he goes where they cannot follow (v.34).
34:
"Ye shall seek me, and shall not find me; and where I am, ye cannot come." The witnesses agree the words point beyond his death to his unapproachable presence with the Father, but divide sharply over what the fruitless seeking is.

The Wesleyan-Arminian and part of the grammatical-historical school read a national seeking. Clarke: "when the Roman armies come against you, you will vainly seek for a deliverer," cut off in your sins, unable to enter the kingdom of glory. Barnes: "ye shall seek the Messiah" — in the nation's calamities they will desire a deliverer and be disappointed; this "does not refer to their individual salvation, but to the deliverance of their nation" (Hosea 5:6; Matthew 24:1ff).

The Reformed voice reads the vain groans of the reprobate. Calvin: Christ speaks of those "whose obstinacy in rejecting Christ had reached the utmost point"; when they "seek" him they do not really seek, for "unbelief and obstinacy hinder them from approaching to God" — like Esau, who groaned and gnashed his teeth over the lost blessing yet "renders himself more unworthy of it" (Genesis 27:38; Hebrews 12:17). The lesson: "receive Christ without delay, while he is still present" (Isaiah 55:6; Isaiah 49:8), "for we know not how long God will bear with our negligence."

The fuller grammatical-historical reading widens it past any single event. Ellicott connects the words with 8:21 and 13:33 (where they are applied to the disciples), and so excludes "any special reference, such as to the destruction of Jerusalem": they refer rather "to the more general truth... that the time was at hand when he would return to the Father, and his bodily presence would be unapproachable, alike by those who should seek in hatred, or those who should seek in love." Robertson agrees there is no conflict with v.33: "where I am" is Christ's "eternal spiritual home... in fellowship with the Father," beyond their comprehension. Wesley notes these words are "the text which is commented upon in this and the following chapter," and that "where I am" intimates Christ's "perpetual presence" in heaven by his divine nature. So the debate stands: vain national or reprobate seeking (Clarke, Barnes, Calvin) versus a general truth of his unapproachable ascended presence (Ellicott, Robertson).
35:
"Whither will he go that we shall not find him? Will he go unto the Dispersion among the Greeks, and teach the Greeks?" The witnesses read the Jews' literal-minded guess as an unconscious prophecy of the Gentile mission.

The grammatical-historical readers expound the term and the irony. Ellicott's long note explains the diaspora — the Jews scattered from Babylonia, Egypt, and Syria "in every part of the civilised world," a "network of Judaism" linked to Jerusalem; the rulers ask in scorn whether this Rabbi will leave the centre of learning "and become a teacher of the very heathen themselves." Yet their words are, "like the words of Caiaphas" (John 11:49-51), "an unconscious prophecy," summing up "the method of procedure in the earliest mission-work of the church" — "every apostolic church of the Gentiles may be said to have grown out of a synagogue of the Jews" (Acts 15:21). Robertson: they "thus ignorantly anticipating the course Christianity took; what seemed unlikely and impossible to them became actual." Barnes notes Jesus "returned no answer," turning minds from the place of his going to "the great affairs of their own personal salvation." Lightfoot weighs whether "Greeks" means scattered Greeks or Jews scattered among them.

The Reformed voice reads it as proof of their stupidity. Calvin: Christ "spoke expressly of the Father, but they remain fixed on the earth," thinking only of "a departure to distant countries"; the "dispersion" denotes not the uncircumcised but "the Jews who were dispersed," as Peter and James address the strangers of the dispersion (1 Peter 1:1; James 1:1). They may even "teaze Christ by this mockery" — will he fix his reign in Greece? — "and yet the severe threatening which Christ had uttered did not at all affect them."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley keeps both senses open: the Jews scattered in Greece, "or, will he teach the Greeks? — the heathens themselves." Across the schools the verse is a study in spiritual deafness that, in God's irony, blurts out the truth: he would indeed go to the Gentiles — not in person, Augustine had said at v.31, but by his members.
36:
A short verse: the Jews repeat his saying — "Ye shall seek me, and shall not find me; and where I am, ye cannot come" — puzzling over words they cannot fathom. The witnesses comment briefly, reading their wonder as the measure of their blindness.

The grammatical-historical readers note the shift from scorn to bafflement. Ellicott: "their scorn does not solve their difficulty, and gives place to wonder. They feel his words cannot mean what they have said. What, then, do they mean?" Robertson: "puzzled and uneasy over this unintelligible saying," adding that even Peter is later distressed over the same words (13:37). Gill marks that the saying "is not easy to be understood.". Robertson adds that the very phrasing they echo will return to trouble even a disciple, for Peter too will one day be perplexed by Christ's talk of going where they cannot follow (John 13:36).

The verse functions, across the witnesses, as a hinge: the rulers' repetition shows the words have struck home without being grasped, the very repetition (the Evangelist's device) underscoring that the meaning lies beyond the reach of minds fixed on "distant lands" (v.35).
