It is the same incomprehension that runs through the chapter — they know the surface and miss the Father (v.28) — now turned upon the riddle of his departure. (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown in the merged sources here treats another scene and does not bear on this verse.) The unanswered question hangs over the discourse until, on the feast's last day, Christ cries the great invitation (v.37).

The witnesses leave the verse, then, as a study in self-defeating cleverness: the rulers can repeat Christ's words exactly yet cannot enter their meaning, because they will not lift their minds above "distant lands" to the Father from whom he came and to whom he goes. Their baffled "What is this word?" is the chapter's recurring verdict on unbelief — near enough to quote the truth, too far to receive it (v.28) — and it leaves the great invitation of the feast's last day (v.37) standing over their confusion.
37:
"In the last day, that great day of the feast, Jesus stood and cried, If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink." This is the chapter's summit, and the witnesses lavish comment on it — the setting, the cry, the thirst, and the free invitation.

The Eastern and Latin fathers expound the scene. Chrysostom: with the feast of Tabernacles ending and the people about to return home, "our Lord gives them provisions for the way"; he waited for the last and "great" day, when all were gathered, and "cried" because of the multitude, using "no compulsion or violence — but if any have the desire strong enough, let him come." Augustine: "there is an inner thirst, because there is an inner man"; "if we thirst, let us come, not by our feet but by our affections, not by change of place but by love." Both ground the appeal in the beatitude's hunger and thirst after righteousness (Matthew 5:6).

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers fix the setting and the freeness. Gill and Lightfoot detail the eighth day's diminishing sacrifices (Numbers 29) and the Jews' esteem of it as "the great day." Ellicott and Robertson note "cried" implies intensity (1:15; 12:44). Calvin: by "if any man thirst" Christ "exhorts us to come with desire," excluding the satisfied and self-sufficient; the invitation is free, "I use no compulsion" — yet only the thirsty come. The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke and Wesley keep the open call: all who feel their need may drink.

The Puritan Henry calls it "a gospel invitation to come to Christ, and a gospel promise of comfort," given on the last day because "much people were gathered" and "the people were now returning to their homes" (Proverbs 1:20). On the punctuation, Chrysostom and Augustine (with the moderns) place the stop after "as the Scripture hath said," so that "out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water" is Christ's own assertion — grounded not in any one text but, Jerome suggests, in Proverbs 5:16 and the prophets, and the thirst-and-water imagery of John 4:14 and Isaiah 55:1. "Belly," the fathers agree, means the heart; the rivers are the overflow of the Spirit's grace into love of neighbour (Psalm 40:8).
38:
"He that believeth on me... out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water." The witnesses read the promise as the overflowing abundance of the Spirit's grace in the believer — first received, then flowing out to others.

The Eastern and Latin fathers unfold the image. Chrysostom: "belly" means the heart (Psalm 40:8); "rivers, not river," shows "the copious and overflowing power of grace," and "living" means "ever working" — like a spring that "faileth not, becometh not empty," as seen in "the wisdom of Stephen, the tongue of Peter, the strength of Paul." Augustine: "the belly of the inner man is the heart's conscience"; he who drinks "becomes the very fountain itself," and the river that flows out is "the love of his neighbour" — "if he does good to his neighbour, the stream is not dried up, but flows." Gregory and Alcuin (in the Catena) add that "rivers of living water" run down when "sacred preaching flows from the soul of the faithful."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers anchor the promise in Scripture and the Spirit. Robertson and Ellicott note the free citation, paralleling "a well of water springing up" (John 4:14). Calvin: the believer is so filled that the grace "overflows" to others; Christ "does not wish believers to be like dry and barren soil, but to water their neighbours." Lightfoot weighs the Old Testament backgrounds. The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke and Wesley read the indwelling Spirit poured out in abundance.

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem ties it explicitly to the Spirit: "this spake he of the Spirit, which they that believe on him should receive" — "not rivers perceived by sense... but bringing souls to the light" (John 4:14). So the witnesses converge: the believer who drinks of Christ becomes himself a channel, the Spirit's grace within him so abundant that it cannot but flow out — the very dynamic the next verse will name as the gift of the Spirit (v.39).

The witnesses also note how the verse turns reception into mission: the believer is not a cistern that merely holds but a spring that gives, the Spirit's grace within him so full that it must overflow to others. Augustine's reading — that the outflowing river is love of neighbour — binds the promise to the life of the church, and Cyril's — that this is "the Spirit which they that believe should receive" — binds it to the next verse, where the Evangelist names the gift outright and ties it to the glorifying of Christ (v.39).
39:
The Evangelist interprets: "This spake he of the Spirit, which they that believe on him should receive; for the Holy Ghost was not yet given, because that Jesus was not yet glorified." The witnesses read it as the key to the living water — and weigh the puzzling "not yet given."

The Latin father gives the fullest account. Augustine: "the Spirit of God was" — was with God — "before now; but was not yet given to those who believed on Jesus," for the Lord had determined not to give the Spirit "till he was risen again." The Spirit was indeed in the prophets, in the Baptist, in Zacharias and Mary and Simeon; but "the giving of the Holy Spirit was to be certain, after Christ's exaltation, in a way it never was before" — with the new sign of tongues. Why after the resurrection? "That the flame of love might mount upwards to our own resurrection," Christ first showing "that life which we hope to attain." Chrysostom (in the Catena) adds that the gift waited on the cross: "gifts are not made to enemies, but to friends... the victim must first be offered up."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers fix the meaning of "not yet given." Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson: the words mean not the absolute absence of the Spirit but his not yet being given "in that full and copious manner" reserved for Pentecost. Calvin: the gifts of the Spirit were always in the Church, but the abundant, manifest outpouring waited on Christ's glorification — for the Spirit's full office is to apply the redemption then accomplished. Gill notes the Spirit was given in measure before, but the "extraordinary" pouring out waited.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley and the Eastern Cyril keep the plain sense: the living water is the Spirit, received by believers, and given in fullness only after Jesus is glorified. So the witnesses agree the verse defines the promise of v.38 and ties the gift of the Spirit to the finished work of Christ — the cross, resurrection, and exaltation that v.33 had foreshadowed.

The witnesses thus make the verse the hinge of the whole invitation: the living water is no vague blessing but the person and gift of the Holy Spirit, and its full outpouring waits upon the cross, resurrection, and exaltation of Christ. Augustine's care to affirm the Spirit's prior working — in prophets, in the Baptist, in Mary — guards against the error that there was no Spirit before; the newness lies in the manner and measure of Pentecost, the flame of love poured out once the Son is glorified (v.37).
40:
"Many of the people therefore... said, Of a truth this is the Prophet. Others said, This is the Christ." The cry of v.37 provokes a threefold split, and the witnesses read the divided verdict that the chapter has been building toward.

The Latin father reads the contest of opinion. Augustine (in the Catena): some take a "low way of speaking, after the manner of the multitude," while others rightly own him; the crowd is sifted by the word. He distinguishes "the Prophet" (the one promised in John 1:21) from "the Christ," as the legates had at the start.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers mark the categories. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson note that "the Prophet" was expected as a distinct figure (after Deuteronomy 18:15), and that some now advance to confess him as Messiah outright. Robertson: these "went further and dared to call Jesus the Messiah." Wesley keeps it plain.

The Reformed and Puritan voices draw the lesson of division. Calvin (anticipated here, fully at v.43): "Christ's sermon produced a schism, and that not among Gentiles... but in the midst of the Church"; yet "divisions do not properly draw their origin from the Gospel," for the seed of difference "lay concealed in men" and is only awakened, "as vapours are produced... till the sun arises." Henry: the discourses "convinced many that he was the Messiah; but they had not courage to own it." So the witnesses agree the verse opens the chapter's climactic division — Prophet, Christ, or impostor from Galilee — that the next verses will sharpen into open schism (v.43).

The witnesses leave the verse as the formal opening of the chapter's climactic division: the crowd, stirred by the cry of the feast's last day, sorts itself into those who own him as the Prophet, those who confess him the Christ, and those who will object from Galilee. Augustine's note that the word itself does the sifting, and Calvin's that the discord was latent in men before the gospel awoke it, together prepare the reader for the open schism and the rulers' rage that follow (v.43, 45).
41:
"Others said, This is the Christ. But some said, Shall Christ come out of Galilee?" The witnesses read the deepening of the division: a bolder party now confesses him Messiah, while others raise the geographical objection that will dominate to the chapter's end.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the two parties. Robertson: these "went further and dared to call Jesus the Messiah... they said it openly," while the objectors give "as their reason (for) the fact that he came from Galilee," the form of the question expecting the answer "No." Ellicott marks the Messiah distinguished from "the Prophet" (John 1:20-21), and renders the objection, "Surely the Messiah cometh not out of Galilee?"

The Reformed voice draws the lesson of controversy and the root of their error. Calvin: "Christ's sermon produced a schism," and "we ought not to think it strange in the present day if men are divided" — yet the division "in the chief seat of the Church" is no reproach to the gospel, "for there can be no firm agreement among men except in undoubted truth." The objectors "fortify themselves by the testimony of Scripture," and "have some appearance of truth"; their one error is that "they make Christ a Galilean," for "if they had taken the trouble to inquire, they would have seen" he was both of Bethlehem and of David — "out of the Scriptures themselves, which lead us by the hand to Christ, men frequently make obstacles for themselves."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke presses the same point pastorally: had they "examined the matter a little farther, they would have found that he had his birth exactly as the prophets had foretold; but, for want of this necessary examination, they continued in unbelief" — "many still lose their souls nearly in the same way," led away by "common report" and refusing a fair hearing. Gill notes the believing party reasoned not only from the miracles but from the promised "rivers of living water" and "waters in the wilderness" of the Messianic prophecies (Isaiah 35:6). So the witnesses agree: the objection had a surface plausibility, undone only by an inquiry the objectors would not make.
42:
"Hath not the Scripture said, That Christ cometh of the seed of David, and out of the town of Bethlehem?" The witnesses read the objection as a true premise wrongly applied — Scripture rightly cited, but in ignorance of where Jesus was actually born.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the texts and the irony. Barnes and Robertson cite the prophecies of Davidic descent and the Bethlehem birth (Micah 5:2; Psalm 132:11; 1 Samuel 16:1,4). Robertson notes "this ignorance of the fact that Jesus was actually born in Bethlehem belongs to the Jews, not to John." Ellicott meets the old objection that John records the questions "without a correction": he "is giving the feelings and opinions of the multitude," and "it is a mark of the truthfulness of his narrative that he gives them just as they really occurred" — a second-century writer could not have so thrown himself into "the spirit of a Jewish multitude."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley sharpens the irony to a point: "from Bethlehem — and how could they forget that Jesus was born there? Had not Herod given them terrible reason to remember it?" (Micah 5:2). Gill notes these objectors "were accounted the more wise and knowing," conversant with the Scriptures, which makes their failure the more culpable.

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem turns the Davidic prophecy to doctrine: God "sware in truth unto David" (Psalm 132:11; Psalm 89:36-37) of a seed and throne enduring "as the sun" — fulfilled not in Solomon, whose throne did not so endure, but in Christ; "David's throne" means "not the throne of wood, but the kingdom itself." (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown in the merged sources here treats the widow's mite and does not bear on this verse.) So the witnesses agree: the objectors held the right Scripture and drew the wrong conclusion, lacking only the fact of Bethlehem that lay within their reach.

The witnesses thus turn the verse into a lesson on knowledge without inquiry: the objectors held the right prophecy yet drew the wrong conclusion, lacking only a fact — the birth at Bethlehem — that lay within their reach. The irony, which Ellicott presses, is that John records their error without correction precisely because he is faithfully reporting how the crowd reasoned; the reader, who knows the genealogies and the flight from Herod, sees that the Scripture they wielded against Christ in fact testified for him (Micah 5:2).
43:
"So there was a division among the people because of him." A brief verse, and the witnesses read it as the fulfilment of Christ's own word and the recurring mark of his ministry in John.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the word. Robertson and Ellicott note the term is schisma, "a clear split" — found in the earlier Gospels only of a literal rent (Matthew 9:16; Mark 2:21), but used by John to mark "this rent into two parties of the Jewish multitude," here and at 9:16 and 10:19; later "both the word and the fact... passed into the history of the Church, where the same word names the rending of congregations (1 Corinthians 11:18)" (1 Corinthians 1:10). Clarke: "a schism; they were divided in sentiment, and separated into parties. This is the true notion of schism."

The Reformed voice (Gill) reads the threefold split — some taking him for a prophet, some confessing him the Christ, some objecting from Galilee — as the express fulfilment of Christ's prediction, so that the division itself witnesses to his word. Calvin's fuller treatment (given at v.41) governs here: the schism springs not from the gospel but from the hidden discord in men, awakened by the light.

The witnesses thus leave the verse as a sober refrain: wherever Christ is truly preached, the crowd divides — not because the word sows discord, but because it exposes what was already there. The same split will harden in the next verse into the impulse to seize him (v.44), even as it stays the hand that would have done it.

The witnesses also let the verse stand as a quiet vindication of Christ's own word, who had foretold that his coming would set men at variance. The division is not the gospel's failure but its effect — the light disclosing what the darkness had hidden — and the same split that here merely murmurs will, in the next verse, arm some with the wish to seize him while, under God, leaving his hour untouched (v.44).
44:
"And some of them would have taken him; but no man laid hands on him." The witnesses read the restrained arrest as a second display (after v.30) of God's providence guarding Christ until his hour.

The grammatical-historical readers note the grammar and the cause. Robertson: the imperfect ēthelon, "were wishing to seize him," a like impulse and restraint as in v.30. Ellicott identifies the "some" with those who had asked "Doth the Christ come out of Galilee?" (v.41), distinct from the officers (v.32); the restraint is explained "in part by the existence of a section who received him," and "in part by the power of his presence and words," which impressed even the officers (John 18:6).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the providence out: "they wished to seize him, and... would have destroyed him too at that time, had they been unanimous; but their being divided in opinion (v.43) was the cause, under God, why his life was at that time preserved" — "the wrath of man shall praise thee; and the remainder thereof thou wilt restrain" (Psalm 76:10).

The Reformed voice agrees. Calvin: their rejection "was accompanied by cruelty and eagerness to do him injury, for superstition is always cruel"; that their efforts failed "we ought to ascribe to the providence of God," since Christ, his hour not yet come, "surmounted all dangers." Gill names the violent party who would carry him before the Sanhedrin "as an impostor and blasphemer." So the witnesses converge: the very division that condemned him also, under God, shielded him — the wrath of men made to serve the appointed hour.

The witnesses thus read the verse as the chapter's second proof that no hand can fall on Christ before the appointed hour: the will to seize him is present and violent, but it is checked — by the divided crowd, by the power of his presence, and behind both by the providence of God. Clarke's appeal to Psalm 76:10 and Calvin's to the decree of the Father converge: the wrath of men is real, but it is held on a leash until it is permitted to serve, not thwart, the purpose of redemption (v.30).
45:
"Then came the officers to the chief priests and Pharisees; and they said unto them, Why have ye not brought him?" The witnesses read the empty-handed return of the temple police as a quiet triumph of Christ's word over the schemes of the rulers.

The Eastern father seizes the irony. Chrysostom: "nothing is clearer, nothing simpler than the truth, if we deal not perversely" — the Scribes and Pharisees, "ever with Christ for the sake of plotting," were "nothing profited, but even harmed," while the officers, "who could not claim one of these privileges, were subdued by one single sermon." To "come" empty-handed "was a far greater deed than to have remained," for now "they become heralds of the wisdom of Christ" and show "their boldness" before "the Pharisees, his enemies."

The Puritan Henry paints the scene: the chief priests and Pharisees, who "attended not the religious services of the day" but sat "in the council-chamber, expecting Christ to be brought a prisoner," reprove their officers — "mischievous men fret that they cannot do the mischief they would" (Psalm 112:10; Nehemiah 6:16). It "vexed them that those who were their own creatures" should disappoint them.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers fix the setting. Robertson and Ellicott note the officers (the hypēretai of v.32) return to the assembled Sanhedrin. Gill observes they "were not on the side of those who were for seizing him," but "rather took part with those who affirmed he was the Messiah," or at least an extraordinary prophet. Calvin reads the officers' restraint as God's hand on the consciences of unwilling men. So the witnesses agree: the warrant of v.32 returns void, the would-be captors captured by the word.

The witnesses leave the scene heavy with irony: the rulers who skipped the worship of the feast to sit in judgment find their own warrant returned void, their hired officers half-won to the prisoner they were sent to fetch. Chrysostom's marvel at the officers' boldness and Henry's picture of the fretting council together set up the testimony that follows — "Never man spake like this man" (v.46) — a confession the more damning for coming from the rulers' own servants.
46:
"Never man spake like this man." The witnesses dwell on the officers' testimony as one of Scripture's great involuntary tributes — drawn from hostile men by the sheer power of Christ's word.

The Puritan Henry gives the fullest weight. "This was a very great truth, that never any man spake with that wisdom, and power, and grace, that convincing clearness, and that charming sweetness, wherewith Christ spoke; none of the prophets, no, not Moses himself." "The very officers that were sent to take him were taken with him"; though men "who had no inclination to think well of Jesus, yet so much self-evidence was there in what Christ said" that they preferred him "before all those that sat in Moses's seat" — "thus Christ was preserved by the power God has upon the consciences even of bad men," and their witness "to their lords and masters" became "a vexation of their sin and an aggravation of their sin."

The Eastern father marks what the officers did not say. Chrysostom: "we must marvel at their understanding, that they needed not signs, but were taken by the teaching alone" — "they said not, Never man wrought miracles thus, but, Never man spake thus." When "the mind is impartial, there is no need of long arguments. Such a thing is truth."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers keep it brief but agree. Barnes and Robertson read the candid confession of men sent to arrest him; Calvin sees the word's self-authenticating force on the conscience. Across the schools the verse stands as the answer to the rulers' "Why have ye not brought him?" (v.45): they could not, because the word had disarmed them — a testimony the more telling for coming from the very men commissioned to silence it.

The witnesses thus make the officers' word the chapter's pivot: pressed to explain why they came empty-handed, they can only testify to the unique power of Christ's speech. Henry's reading — that God preserved Christ "by the power he has upon the consciences even of bad men" — and Chrysostom's — that impartial hearers needed no miracle, only the teaching — together frame the verse as an involuntary tribute that turns the rulers' interrogation into a witness against themselves (v.45).
47:
"Are ye also deceived?" The Pharisees turn on their own officers, and the witnesses read the sneer as the standard weapon of pride against a spreading faith.

The Puritan Henry expounds the tactic. The Pharisees "endeavour to secure their officers... and to beget in them prejudices against Christ": first, that "if they embrace the gospel of Christ they will deceive themselves" — "Christianity has, from its first rise, been represented to the world as a great cheat," though "the event declares that none were ever more shamefully deceived... than those who promised themselves worldly wealth and secular dominion with the Messiah." The flattery is calculated: "Are you also deceived? What! men of your sense... that know better than to be imposed upon by every upstart teacher?" — they "endeavour to prejudice them against Christ by persuading them to think well of themselves."

The Reformed voice reads the contempt. Calvin (whose treatment runs through vv.48–49): the rulers, "relying on the title of Priests," wish "to subject all to them in a tyrannical manner," and an "immoderate love of ourselves is accompanied by contempt of the brethren." Gill notes the scornful "ye also" — as if the officers had joined the deluded rabble.

The grammatical-historical readers keep it spare. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson read the rhetorical question expecting indignant denial, the rulers assuming that none of sense or standing could be drawn to the Galilean. So the witnesses agree: unable to answer "Never man spake like this man" (v.46), the Pharisees resort to ridicule and the appeal to vanity — the argument of those who have no argument, which the next verses press into outright contempt for the people (v.49).

The witnesses thus leave the verse as the reflex of cornered pride: having no answer to "Never man spake like this man" (v.46), the Pharisees fall back on ridicule and flattery, branding faith as self-deception and appealing to the officers' sense of their own importance. Henry's irony — that the truly deceived were those who looked for a Messiah of worldly pomp — and Calvin's diagnosis of priestly self-love together expose the sneer as the argument of those who have run out of arguments (v.48).
48:
"Have any of the rulers or of the Pharisees believed on him?" The witnesses read the appeal to authority and rank as the second prong of the Pharisees' attack — and one history would soon refute.

The Puritan Henry exposes the logic: the officers are urged to consider that to become disciples "they go contrary to those who were persons of quality and reputation" — "will you be wiser than they?" Yet "some of the rulers did embrace Christ" (Matthew 9:18; John 4:53), and the very next verses produce one — Nicodemus (v.50). "Most men, even in their religion, are willing to be governed by the example of those of the first rank," and the Pharisees play on it.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers note the irony. Barnes and Ellicott observe the boast was even then untrue, and that God "hath chosen the weak things" (1 Corinthians 1:26-27) — the faith of the despised multitude would outlast the unbelief of the rulers. Wesley keeps it plain.

The Reformed voice ties it to their pride. Calvin: the rulers measure truth by their own rank, "as those who excessively flatter themselves are always disposed to abuse others"; what they offer as proof — that no authority believes — is in truth the symptom of their blindness. (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown in the merged sources here treats another scene and does not bear on this verse.) So the witnesses agree: the argument from authority was both false in fact and worthless in principle, and the chapter at once supplies its living contradiction in Nicodemus.

The witnesses thus read the appeal to rank as both false and self-condemning: false, because the chapter at once produces a believing ruler in Nicodemus (v.50), and the despised crowd's faith would outlast the leaders' unbelief; self-condemning, because it measures truth by the standing of its adherents rather than by its evidence. Calvin's note that pride "abuses others" and Henry's that men follow "those of the first rank" together explain why the question carried weight — and why God answered it by choosing the weak things of the world (1 Corinthians 1:27).
49:
"But this people who knoweth not the law are cursed." The witnesses read the Pharisees' verdict on the common people as a display of contempt that usurps God's prerogative.

The grammatical-historical readers supply the rabbinic background. Lightfoot, Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson explain the term: the leaders scorned the untutored crowd as the 'am hâ-âretz, "people of the earth," set against those instructed in the Law, the "holy people"; the Rabbinic writings are "full of scorn" for them — "No brutish man is sin-fearing, nor is one of the people of the earth pious." Barnes: the word for "people" is one of contempt, "the rabble"; they assumed any who believed on the despised Jesus must be "ignorant of the true doctrines." Robertson recalls the Sanhedrin's later amazement at Peter and John as "unlettered" men (Acts 4:13), and that "the common people heard Jesus gladly" (Mark 12:37).

The Puritan Henry presses the injustice. They "reproach them as ignorant of the word of God," as if "no scripture-knowledge were current but what came out of their mint"; yet "many a plain, honest, unlearned disciple of Christ... attains to a more clear, sound, and useful knowledge of the word of God" than the learned (Psalm 119:99-100) — and if the people were ignorant, whose fault but the teachers' who "took away the key of knowledge" (Luke 11:52)? To pronounce them "cursed" is "a usurpation of God's prerogative," for "the curse causeless shall not come" (Proverbs 26:2).

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices name the hidden motive. Calvin: they alleged the people's ignorance, but "another reason, which they concealed, was that they thought there was no holiness but in their own rank" — as "the Popish priests in our own day pretend that none but themselves deserve to be called the Church." Wesley: the ignorant rabble are "by that ignorance exposed to the curse of being thus seduced." So the witnesses agree: the Pharisees' curse fell on those whom Christ was gathering, and condemned chiefly themselves.

The witnesses thus turn the Pharisees' curse back upon themselves: the people they damn as ignorant of the law are the very ones Christ is gathering, while the self-styled guardians of the law are about to break it (v.51). Henry's protest that cursing a whole people "usurps God's prerogative," and Calvin's exposure of the hidden claim that holiness dwells only in their own rank, together mark this contempt as the temper that most surely shuts men out of the kingdom the unlettered crowd was entering (Mark 12:37).
50:
"Nicodemus saith unto them, (he that came to Jesus by night, being one of them)..." The witnesses read the appearance of Nicodemus as God raising a witness within the hostile council itself.

The grammatical-historical readers note his courage and his place. Robertson: "now he boldly protests against the injustice of condemning Jesus unheard" — a Pharisee and member of the Sanhedrin, "his present act is courageous," and he answers their own challenge of v.48, "being one of them." Ellicott: his position is "that of a friend of Jesus, who still does not dare to declare himself His open follower" (John 3:2). Barnes: "God often places one or more pious men in legislative assemblies to vindicate his honour," and one "at one time timid and fearful" may "on other occasions be bold" (John 3:1).

The Puritan Henry weighs his retaining his seat: some impute it to "weakness and cowardice," but "Christ had never said to him, Follow me," and there "he might have opportunity of serving Christ and stemming the tide of the Jewish rage," as "Hushai among Absalom's counsellors" or "Daniel in Nebuchadnezzar's court"; though "at first he came by night, for fear... when there was occasion, he boldly appeared in defence of Christ" (John 19:39).

The Reformed voice is more measured. Calvin describes him as "a neutral man, who does not venture to undertake in good earnest the defence of sound doctrine, and yet cannot endure to have the truth oppressed" — "some small spark of godliness shines from his heart," yet "in not defending Christ openly, he manifests excessive timidity." The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads him "having now a little more courage." So the witnesses agree: Nicodemus is a half-formed disciple, but God uses even his hesitant word to break the council's rush to judgment (v.51).

The witnesses thus hold Nicodemus in a careful balance: neither the bold confessor he will later become at the burial (John 19:39), nor a mere coward, but a man with a true if timid spark of faith whom God uses at the decisive moment. Henry's defence of his remaining in the council and Calvin's frank naming of his "excessive timidity" together let the reader see grace at work in an unfinished disciple — enough courage, at least, to ask the one question that will halt the rush to condemn (v.51).
51:
"Doth our law judge any man, before it hear him, and know what he doeth?" The witnesses read Nicodemus's question as a just appeal to the law's own first principle of fair trial, turned shrewdly against the rulers.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the legal ground. Barnes: the law required justice and forbade condemning the unheard (Leviticus 19:15; Exodus 23:1-2; Deuteronomy 19:15,18); "every man should be presumed innocent until proved guilty." Robertson: these "exponents of the law (v.49) were really violating the law of criminal procedure" (Exodus 23:1; Deuteronomy 1:16); Nicodemus "could at least show his colours and score the point of justice." Clarke cites Josephus that "the law has forbidden any man to be put to death... unless first condemned by the Sanhedrin." Ellicott: "this Law, which we do know and understand, doth it judge without open investigation?" — "this holy people, instructed in the Law, were the Law-breakers."

The Puritan Henry admires the prudence. Nicodemus "argues from the principles of their own law... that no man is to be condemned unheard"; he wisely waives "the excellency of Christ's doctrine or the evidence of his miracles," which would have been "to cast pearls before swine," and retorts upon the rulers their own charge of ignorance of the law (v.49) — "so unfit were they to give law to others."

The witnesses thus agree: Nicodemus pleads not Christ's cause directly but the law's plainest rule, exposing that the men who scorned the people as ignorant of the law were themselves about to break it. The appeal is modest — "however feeble," Calvin had said (v.50) — yet enough to halt the council and provoke the scornful retort of the next verse (v.52).

The witnesses thus read Nicodemus's plea as a masterstroke of restraint: he defends not Christ's person but the law's plainest rule, so that the men who had just scorned the people as ignorant of the law are convicted of breaking it. Barnes's appeal to the statutes of fair trial and Henry's praise of his prudence in waiving Christ's miracles together show why the modest question proved so effective — it gave the rulers nothing to attack but justice itself, and so drew only the sneer of the next verse (v.52).
52:
"Art thou also of Galilee? Search, and look: for out of Galilee ariseth no prophet." The witnesses read the rulers' reply as scorn in place of argument — and, on the facts, simply false.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers expose the error. Robertson: "as a matter of fact Jonah, Hosea, Nahum, possibly also Elijah, Elisha, and Amos were from Galilee"; the claim "was simply the rage of the Sanhedrin against Jesus regardless of the facts." Wesley: "they could not but know the contrary" — Jonah arose from Gath-hepher (2 Kings 14:25), Nahum from a Galilean village, "and Thisbe, the town of Elijah, was in Galilee also" — and they knew too that Jesus was born in Bethlehem; "conscious this poor answer would not bear examination," they cut off reply. Barnes: "ridicule is not argument, and there is no demonstration in a gibe," yet "this is the only weapon which the proud often used." Lightfoot hears it spoken "scoffingly: Art thou of those Galileans that believe in this Galilean?"

The Reformed voice reads their fury. Calvin: "the extreme violence to which they are excited against Nicodemus shows with what furious hatred they burned against Christ"; he "had not avowedly undertaken to defend Christ," yet "immediately the enemies fly into a passion, and exclaim that he is a heretic" — "thus among the Papists in our own day, no man can show the slightest token of candour... but immediately the enemies fly into a passion."

Ellicott notes they "seek to avoid his question, to which there could have been but one answer, by a counter-question," passing "from the matter of fact... to the question of authority." So the witnesses agree: pressed by a just legal point, the rulers answer with a sneer and a falsehood, betraying that they had neither argument nor candour, only the will to be rid of him.

The witnesses thus leave the rulers exposed on every side: their retort is a sneer, not an argument; it is factually false, since prophets had indeed arisen from Galilee; and it betrays the very fury Nicodemus's mild appeal was meant to cool. Wesley's catalogue of Galilean prophets and Calvin's reading of their rage as proof of their hatred of Christ together show that, pressed by a just question, the guardians of the law could answer only with contempt and a falsehood — the surest sign that they had judged before they had heard (v.51).
53:
"And every man went unto his own house." The witnesses read the council's dissolution as a providential collapse — and the grammatical commentators pause to flag the textual question that opens here.

The grammatical-historical readers raise the text honestly. Robertson: this verse through 8:12 (the woman taken in adultery) "is certainly not a genuine part of John's Gospel" — absent from the oldest and best manuscripts, first appearing in Codex Bezae, placed by some at the Gospel's end or in Luke; "it is probably a true story, for it is like Jesus, but it does not belong to John's Gospel." Ellicott agrees it is "one of the most striking instances of an undoubted addition," belonging "to the Apostolic age" but "not... from the pen of St. John," an insertion that "breaks the order of the discourse." Clarke notes the passage is "wanting in several MSS." and marked with obelisks, with great variety of reading.

The Reformed voice reads the providence in the scattering. Calvin: weighing "the reign of the priests at that time, with what rage they were excited," against Christ "unarmed and defenceless," "the conclusion must be that it was all over with him a hundred times"; when "so formidable a conspiracy is dissolved of its own accord... who will not acknowledge that they were scattered by the hand of God?" Barnes sees "every mark of confusion and disorder," the Sadducees perhaps joining Nicodemus, "a most instructive exhibition of the influence of pride, envy, contempt, and anger."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley marvels at the instrument: "that short plain question of Nicodemus spoiled all their measures, and broke up the council! A word spoken in season, how good it is!" (Proverbs 15:23). So the witnesses agree on the providence — one just question, under God, dissolving the plot — while honestly noting that the verse also marks the seam where a beloved but non-Johannine narrative was later joined.

The witnesses thus close the council scene on two notes at once: the providence that scattered a deadly conspiracy by one just question, and the textual seam where a beloved but non-Johannine narrative was later joined. Calvin's wonder at the plot dissolving "of its own accord," and the grammatical commentators' candour about the manuscripts, together let the reader honour both the history and the text — receiving the breaking-up of the council as the hand of God (Proverbs 21:30), while reading what follows with open eyes about its origin.
